channel.title: NPR Topics: Economy
channel.description: NPR news on the U.S. and world economy, the World Bank, and Federal Reserve. Commentary on economic trends. Subscribe to NPR Economy podcasts and RSS feeds.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: U.S. gasoline prices set to jump with Iran war
item.description: The U.S. war with Iran is rattling energy and financial markets. Gasoline prices jumped overnight while stock prices tumbled.
item.content:encoded: The U.S. war with Iran is rattling energy and financial markets. Gasoline prices jumped overnight while stock prices tumbled.
item.dc:creator: Scott Horsley

item.title: The candy heir vs. chocolate skimpflation
item.description: The grandson of the Reese's Peanut Butter Cups creator has launched a campaign against The Hershey Company, which owns the Reese's brand. He wants them to stop skimping on ingredients.
item.content:encoded: The grandson of the Reese's Peanut Butter Cups creator has launched a campaign against The Hershey Company, which owns the Reese's brand. He wants them to stop skimping on ingredients.
item.dc:creator: Greg Rosalsky

item.title: Oil prices rise sharply in market trading after attacks in Middle East disrupt supply
item.description: The high prices came as U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran and retaliatory strikes against Israel and U.S. military installations around the Gulf sent disruptions through the global energy supply chain.
item.content:encoded:
The high prices came as U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran and retaliatory strikes against Israel and U.S. military installations around the Gulf sent disruptions through the global energy supply chain.

(Image credit: Kamran Jebreili)
item.dc:creator: The Associated Press

item.title: How military action in Iran — and the broader region — could impact the global economy
item.description: NPR's Ayesha Rascoe talks with Mary Lovely of the Peterson Institute for International Economics about what military action in Iran – and Tehran's response – could mean for the global economy.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Ayesha Rascoe talks with Mary Lovely of the Peterson Institute for International Economics about what military action in Iran – and Tehran's response – could mean for the global economy.
item.dc:creator: Ayesha Rascoe

item.title: These major issues have brought together Democrats and Republicans in states
item.description: Across the country, Republicans and Democrats have found bipartisan agreement on regulating artificial intelligence and data centers. But it's not just big tech aligning the two parties.
item.content:encoded:
Across the country, Republicans and Democrats have found bipartisan agreement on regulating artificial intelligence and data centers. But it's not just big tech aligning the two parties.

(Image credit: Ted Shaffrey)
item.dc:creator: Regan McCarthy

item.title: Why prices won't drop after the Trump tariff ruling, according to economists
item.description: The Supreme Court struck down President Trump's signature tariffs. But the president has other tariff tools, and consumers shouldn't expect cheaper prices anytime soon, economists say.
item.content:encoded:
The Supreme Court struck down President Trump's signature tariffs. But the president has other tariff tools, and consumers shouldn't expect cheaper prices anytime soon, economists say.

(Image credit: Timothy A. Clary)
item.dc:creator: James Doubek

item.title: Is the YIMBY movement doomed?
item.description: For decades, rising home prices have been an engine for middle-class wealth. Now a growing movement wants to slow — or even reverse — that trend. Are the politics around new housing development inherently stacked against them?
item.content:encoded:
For decades, rising home prices have been an engine for middle-class wealth. Now a growing movement wants to slow — or even reverse — that trend. Are the politics around new housing development inherently stacked against them?

(Image credit: Matt Cardy)
item.dc:creator: Greg Rosalsky

item.title: These small-business owners are owed tariff refunds. Will they ever get them?
item.description: Anyone who paid the taxes should get reimbursed, but the high court did not address how. Business owners wonder if they'll need lawyers, brokers, money — or luck.
item.content:encoded:
Anyone who paid the taxes should get reimbursed, but the high court did not address how. Business owners wonder if they'll need lawyers, brokers, money — or luck.

(Image credit: ASM Games)
item.dc:creator: Alina Selyukh

item.title: Trump excoriates Supreme Court justices after tariff loss
item.description: The battle over the tariffs began on day one of the president's second term when he signed an executive order that let him impose a wide range of tariffs on virtually every U.S. trading partner.
item.content:encoded:
The battle over the tariffs began on day one of the president's second term when he signed an executive order that let him impose a wide range of tariffs on virtually every U.S. trading partner.

(Image credit: Kevin Dietsch)
item.dc:creator: Nina Totenberg

item.title: The 'doom loop' helps explain why the global economy is growing bleaker
item.description: How come it feels like it's all bad news in the global economy these days? According to one economist, something he calls the "doom loop."
item.content:encoded: How come it feels like it's all bad news in the global economy these days? According to one economist, something he calls the "doom loop."
item.dc:creator: Darian Woods
